title: Pi latency benchmark stubs BridgePort.call, so it never measures the real per-call Python spawn cost
evidence: benchmark-boundary.ts:40-45 defines the `bridge` object the benchmark wraps builtins with as `call: async () => { bridgeCallCount += 1; return { version: 1, outcome: 'allow' }; }` — an in-memory stub that resolves immediately, never invoking BridgeClient or spawning plugins/ca-pi/hooks/pi-bridge.py. pi_benchmark.py's `_production_pi_samples` (lines 140-180) runs this bundled boundary.mjs and reports its `timings` as the 'pi' host's adapter latency, i.e. the benchmark reports only the JS-side wrapBuiltins dispatch overhead, not the real BridgeClient.call() Python-process-spawn path exercised in production (see performance-001).
impact: The one latency harness that exists for the Pi hot path (per the task's own framing: 'the benchmark harness exists') structurally cannot detect a regression in, or establish a baseline for, the single most expensive step of a real Pi tool call — the per-call Python spawn. A regression there (e.g. adding more imports to pi-bridge.py's hot path, or reverting a future worker-process optimization) would pass this benchmark silently.
recommendation: Add a second benchmark mode that runs the real BridgeClient against the actual pi-bridge.py script (spawning Python, not a stub), so the harness captures end-to-end per-call latency including process-spawn cost; keep the current stubbed mode as an isolated JS-adapter-only baseline but do not present it as 'the Pi host' benchmark without that caveat.
acceptance_criteria: pi_benchmark.py has a mode that measures BridgeClient.call() against the real pi-bridge.py subprocess and reports its own timings distinct from the stubbed adapter-only numbers
CI or docs make clear which number is adapter-only vs end-to-end so a future reader does not mistake the stubbed figure for full per-call latency
